architecture_patterns.name: Layered Architecture
architecture_patterns.description: Organises a system into horizontal layers where each layer has a specific responsibility and may only communicate with adjacent layers. The most common form separates Presentation, Business Logic, and Data Access into distinct tiers.
architecture_patterns.intent: Separate concerns across horizontal layers so each layer can evolve, be tested, and be replaced independently of the others.
architecture_patterns.structure: Layers are stacked vertically. Each layer exposes an interface to the layer above it and depends on the layer below. Requests flow downward; responses flow upward. No layer may skip another layer or communicate laterally with a peer.
components: Presentation Layer — handles user interface or API surface; delegates all business logic downward
Business Logic Layer — owns domain rules and orchestrates use cases; depends on the data layer
Data Access Layer — abstracts persistence; exposes a repository-like interface to the business layer
Database or External Service — the actual persistence store, reached only through the data access layer
advantages: Clear separation of concerns — each layer has one reason to change
Layers can be tested independently using mocks for adjacent layers
New persistence backends can be swapped by replacing the data access layer without touching business logic
Well understood by most engineers — low onboarding cost
Suits most CRUD-heavy business applications
disadvantages: Can degenerate into a God Object at each layer if responsibilities are not carefully bounded
All requests must pass through every layer even when only one layer is involved, adding latency
Strict layer coupling can make cross-cutting concerns such as logging and security awkward
Business logic can leak into the presentation or data layer when discipline slips
when_to_use: Enterprise applications with clear domain boundaries and CRUD-heavy workloads
Teams that need a simple, well-understood structure with low architectural overhead
Applications where the UI, business rules, and database are maintained by different teams
When starting a new project and deferring architectural complexity until the domain is understood
when_not_to_use: When performance is critical and the overhead of passing through all layers on every request is unacceptable
When the domain is complex enough to warrant a richer architecture such as Clean or Hexagonal
When the system must support multiple delivery mechanisms (REST API, CLI, event consumer) simultaneously
examples: Jarvis OS Genesis-011: the desktop UI layer (apps/desktop/) calls JarvisCore, which delegates to the Agent, which calls Skills — a three-layer structure
A typical Django web application: views (presentation) call services (business logic) which call models (data access)
A Spring Boot REST API with Controller, Service, and Repository layers
references: Martin Fowler — Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture (Chapter 1: Layering)
Robert C. Martin — Clean Architecture (Chapter 3)

architecture_patterns.name: Clean Architecture
architecture_patterns.description: Organises a system around the business domain, placing domain entities and use cases at the centre and infrastructure (databases, frameworks, UI) at the outer ring. Dependencies point inward — the domain never depends on infrastructure.
architecture_patterns.intent: Make the business rules the most stable, independently testable part of the system by ensuring all dependencies point toward the domain, never outward toward infrastructure.
architecture_patterns.structure: Four concentric rings: Entities (innermost — enterprise business rules), Use Cases (application business rules), Interface Adapters (controllers, presenters, gateways), and Frameworks and Drivers (outermost — databases, UI, external APIs). The Dependency Rule states that source code dependencies may only point inward.
components: Entities — pure business objects with no framework dependencies; the most stable ring
Use Cases (Interactors) — application-specific business rules; orchestrate entities to fulfil a single task
Interface Adapters — convert data between use case format and external format; include controllers, presenters, and repository implementations
Frameworks and Drivers — all external concerns: web frameworks, databases, UI toolkits, message queues
advantages: Business rules can be tested without any framework, database, or UI present
The entire infrastructure can be swapped without touching a single use case or entity
The architecture explicitly prevents business logic from leaking into infrastructure code
Changes to external frameworks or databases have zero impact on the domain
disadvantages: Higher upfront complexity than layered architecture — more classes, more interfaces, more wiring
Can feel like over-engineering for simple CRUD applications
Requires discipline to maintain the Dependency Rule as the team grows
Data mapping between rings adds boilerplate
when_to_use: Complex domains with rich business rules that must be independently testable
Long-lived applications where infrastructure will change but the domain must remain stable
Applications that must support multiple delivery mechanisms (REST, CLI, event consumer) sharing the same use cases
Teams with the discipline to maintain architectural boundaries over time
when_not_to_use: Simple CRUD applications where the domain has no independent rules worth protecting
Short-lived scripts or prototypes where architectural overhead slows delivery
Small teams where the ceremony of multiple rings adds cognitive load without proportional benefit
examples: Jarvis OS: the Knowledge Engine, Reasoning Engine, and Skills are domain use cases independent of the desktop UI and AI providers — the UI and AI providers are outer-ring infrastructure
A payment processing system where PaymentEntity and ProcessPaymentUseCase have no dependency on Stripe or the database
A healthcare records system where patient domain logic is independent of the EHR vendor's API
references: Robert C. Martin — Clean Architecture: A Craftsman's Guide to Software Structure and Design (2017)
Robert C. Martin — The Clean Architecture (blog.cleancoder.com, 2012)

architecture_patterns.name: Hexagonal Architecture (Ports and Adapters)
architecture_patterns.description: Places the application core at the centre, surrounded by ports (abstract interfaces) through which the application communicates with the outside world. Adapters implement the ports for specific technologies. The application core has no dependency on any specific technology.
architecture_patterns.intent: Allow an application to be driven equally by users, programs, automated tests, or batch scripts, and to be developed and tested in isolation from its eventual run-time devices and databases.
architecture_patterns.structure: The application core defines Ports — interfaces for both driving (left side, primary) and driven (right side, secondary) interactions. Primary adapters drive the application (REST controller, CLI, test harness). Secondary adapters are driven by the application (SQL repository, SMTP mailer, third-party API client). The application core knows only the port interface, never the adapter.
components: Application Core — pure business logic with no framework or technology dependency
Primary Ports — interfaces the application exposes to be driven (e.g. use case interfaces)
Secondary Ports — interfaces the application uses to drive external systems (e.g. repository interfaces)
Primary Adapters — technology-specific implementations that call into primary ports (REST controllers, CLI commands, test harnesses)
Secondary Adapters — technology-specific implementations of secondary ports (SQL repositories, email senders, API clients)
advantages: The application core can be tested completely without any infrastructure using test adapters
Any delivery mechanism (REST, CLI, message queue) can drive the same application core
Infrastructure can be replaced adapter by adapter without touching the core
Technology decisions can be deferred until the domain is understood
disadvantages: More interfaces and adapter classes than layered architecture
The port/adapter distinction requires team discipline to maintain correctly
Data mapping between adapters and the core adds boilerplate
Can feel abstract to developers unfamiliar with the pattern
when_to_use: Applications that must support multiple delivery mechanisms sharing the same business logic
Systems where the infrastructure choices are uncertain and must remain deferrable
When thorough automated testing of business logic is a primary requirement
Applications with complex integrations that must be mockable at the boundary
when_not_to_use: Simple applications with a single delivery mechanism and stable infrastructure choices
When the team is unfamiliar with the pattern and the benefit does not justify the learning cost
CRUD-heavy applications where the application core contains no independent domain logic
examples: Jarvis OS AIManager is a port; OpenAIProvider and AnthropicProvider are secondary adapters — the core never depends on the concrete provider
Jarvis OS VoiceManager is a port; SystemTTSProvider is a secondary adapter — the core speaks through the port
A payments application where the PaymentGatewayPort is implemented by a StripeAdapter and a PayPalAdapter interchangeably
references: Alistair Cockburn — Hexagonal Architecture (alistair.cockburn.us, 2005)
Robert C. Martin — Clean Architecture (Chapter 22)

architecture_patterns.name: Microservices
architecture_patterns.description: Structures an application as a collection of small, independently deployable services. Each service owns a single business capability, runs in its own process, and communicates with other services over a network (typically HTTP or messaging).
architecture_patterns.intent: Enable independent deployment, scaling, and evolution of business capabilities by assigning each capability to a dedicated service with its own data store and deployment pipeline.
architecture_patterns.structure: Each microservice is an autonomous unit: its own codebase, its own database, its own deployment. An API gateway provides a single entry point for clients. Services communicate synchronously (REST, gRPC) or asynchronously (message queues). A service registry or discovery mechanism routes requests between services.
components: Individual Services — each owns one business capability and its data
API Gateway — single entry point; routes, authenticates, and aggregates
Service Registry / Discovery — tracks which service instances are available and where
Message Broker — enables asynchronous communication and event-driven integration between services
Distributed Tracing — observability infrastructure to correlate requests across service boundaries
advantages: Services can be deployed independently — a change to one service does not require redeploying others
Each service can be scaled independently based on its own load profile
Different services can use different technology stacks optimised for their workload
A failure in one service can be isolated from others with circuit breakers
disadvantages: Distributed systems are significantly more complex than monoliths — network failures, latency, and partial failures must be handled explicitly
Data consistency across service boundaries requires eventual consistency patterns rather than ACID transactions
Operational overhead is high — each service needs its own deployment pipeline, monitoring, and logging
Getting service boundaries wrong creates a distributed monolith with all the complexity and none of the benefit
when_to_use: Large systems with distinct business capabilities that need to scale and evolve independently
Organisations structured around autonomous product teams where each team owns its service end-to-end
Systems where different components have radically different scaling requirements
When independent deployment cadences between capabilities are a genuine operational requirement
when_not_to_use: Small teams — the operational overhead of microservices requires significant platform investment
Early-stage products where the domain is not yet understood — premature service decomposition is expensive to reverse
When a Modular Monolith would meet the deployment and scaling requirements at lower complexity
When the team lacks the DevOps maturity to manage distributed systems
examples: A future Jarvis Platform where JarvisCore, the Reasoning Engine, the Knowledge Engine, and the Voice Engine are independent services — each deployable and scalable independently
Netflix: each product capability (catalogue, recommendations, streaming, billing) is a separate service
Amazon: the 'two-pizza team' model where each team owns and operates its own service
references: Sam Newman — Building Microservices: Designing Fine-Grained Systems (2nd ed., 2021)
Martin Fowler — Microservices (martinfowler.com, 2014)

architecture_patterns.name: Modular Monolith
architecture_patterns.description: A single deployable unit structured internally into well-defined, loosely coupled modules, each owning a distinct business capability. Modules communicate through explicit interfaces, not direct imports of each other's internals.
architecture_patterns.intent: Achieve the business capability separation of microservices within a single deployable unit, eliminating distributed systems complexity while preserving the ability to extract services later if required.
architecture_patterns.structure: The application is deployed as one process. Internally, it is divided into modules — each module has its own domain models, business logic, and data access code. Modules expose a public API and treat each other's internals as private. A shared kernel provides only the most stable cross-cutting types.
components: Modules — self-contained business capability units with their own models, logic, and data access
Module Public API — the only permitted entry point for other modules to interact with a given module
Shared Kernel — minimal shared types (IDs, events, common value objects) that all modules depend on
Module Boundary Enforcer — tooling (import-linter, ArchUnit) that prevents cross-module internal imports
advantages: Single deployment unit — no distributed systems complexity, network calls, or eventual consistency
Business capabilities are separated and independently evolvable within the monolith
Much lower operational overhead than microservices
Modules can be extracted into microservices later when genuine need arises
Refactoring within a module is easy; the module boundary prevents accidental coupling
disadvantages: Requires discipline to maintain module boundaries — nothing prevents a developer from importing across module internals without tooling enforcement
A single deployment unit means all modules must be deployed together even when only one changes
Scaling individual capabilities requires scaling the entire application
Technical debt in one module can slow down the whole codebase
when_to_use: Applications that would benefit from business capability separation but do not yet need independent deployment
Teams that want the organisational benefits of microservices without the operational cost
When the domain boundaries are not yet stable enough to commit to service extraction
As the target architecture for a legacy monolith being incrementally refactored
when_not_to_use: When different capabilities have radically different scaling requirements that require independent infrastructure
When organisational autonomy demands that different teams can deploy without coordinating with each other
When the team lacks the discipline to maintain module boundaries without enforcement tooling
examples: Jarvis OS as currently structured: a single deployable Python application whose internals are divided into modules — core/knowledge_engine, core/reasoning, core/skills, core/voice, core/engineering — each with a defined public interface
Shopify: a Rails monolith deliberately structured into bounded contexts with enforced module boundaries
Stack Overflow: a modular monolith serving massive scale before microservices were needed
references: Sam Newman — Monolith to Microservices (Chapter 2: Planning a Migration)
Martin Fowler — Modular Monolith (martinfowler.com, 2019)

architecture_patterns.name: Event-Driven Architecture
architecture_patterns.description: A system where components communicate exclusively through events — immutable records of something that happened. Producers emit events without knowing who will consume them. Consumers react to events without knowing who produced them.
architecture_patterns.intent: Decouple producers and consumers of information so the system can evolve, scale, and react to change without tight temporal or logical coupling between components.
architecture_patterns.structure: An event broker (message queue or event stream) sits between producers and consumers. Producers publish events to topics or queues. Consumers subscribe to topics and process events asynchronously. Events are immutable and represent facts about the past.
components: Event Producers — emit events when something significant happens in their domain
Event Broker — routes and durably stores events (e.g. Kafka, RabbitMQ, AWS SQS)
Event Consumers — subscribe to events and react to them; multiple consumers can react to the same event
Event Schema — the contract defining what each event contains; must be versioned carefully
Dead Letter Queue — stores events that could not be processed for later inspection and replay
advantages: Producers and consumers are fully decoupled — neither knows the other exists
New consumers can be added to react to existing events without modifying producers
Events can be replayed to rebuild state or onboard new consumers with historical data
The system handles bursts gracefully — the broker absorbs load spikes
disadvantages: Eventual consistency — the system does not provide strong consistency guarantees across event boundaries
Debugging is harder — a request no longer follows a synchronous call chain
Event schema evolution requires careful versioning to avoid breaking existing consumers
Ordering guarantees are non-trivial in distributed brokers
when_to_use: Systems where business processes span multiple services that must react to changes in each other
High-throughput workloads where producers should not wait for consumers to complete
When audit trails and event replay are required
When decoupling between producers and consumers is more important than synchronous consistency
when_not_to_use: Simple request-response interactions where synchronous communication is clearer and sufficient
When strong consistency is required and eventual consistency is not acceptable
Small applications where the broker infrastructure cost exceeds the architectural benefit
examples: A future Jarvis Platform where a SessionStartedEvent triggers the Knowledge Engine to load context, the Reasoning Engine to initialise rules, and the Voice Engine to prepare the TTS pipeline — all independently
An e-commerce system where an OrderPlaced event triggers inventory reservation, payment processing, and email notification independently
GitHub Actions: a push event triggers CI pipelines, deployment workflows, and notification systems independently
references: Martin Fowler — Event-Driven Architecture (martinfowler.com)
Sam Newman — Building Microservices (Chapter 4: Communication Styles)

architecture_patterns.name: Client-Server
architecture_patterns.description: Separates the system into two roles: clients that request services, and servers that provide them. Clients initiate requests; servers respond. The server operates independently of the client's internal state.
architecture_patterns.intent: Centralise resources, logic, or data on a server so multiple clients can access them without duplicating the resource, and so the server can be updated without changing every client.
architecture_patterns.structure: Clients and servers are separate processes, typically on separate machines, communicating over a network protocol. The server exposes a well-defined interface. The client knows the server's interface but not its implementation. Multiple clients can connect to a single server simultaneously.
components: Client — initiates requests, presents results to the user or calling system; stateless or maintains local session state
Server — processes requests, manages authoritative data, enforces business rules
Network Protocol — the communication contract between client and server (HTTP, gRPC, WebSocket)
Load Balancer — distributes client requests across multiple server instances for scale
advantages: Resources are centralised — only the server needs to store data, reducing duplication
The server can be updated without touching clients
Multiple client types (web, mobile, CLI) can share a single server
Security can be centralised on the server rather than enforced on every client
disadvantages: The server is a single point of failure unless redundancy is built in
Network latency adds to every interaction
The server must be designed to handle concurrent clients correctly
Client-server coupling through the protocol means breaking API changes require coordinating client updates
when_to_use: When multiple clients need to share data or resources managed by a central authority
When the client device lacks the resources to process or store data locally
When centralised security, logging, and monitoring are required
Web applications, mobile apps with a backend, and any API-driven system
when_not_to_use: Peer-to-peer applications where no central authority is required or desired
Offline-first applications where the client must function without server connectivity
When network latency makes the round-trip cost unacceptable for the use case
examples: Jarvis Android (JarvisApp2) calling the Anthropic and OpenAI APIs — the app is the client, the AI service is the server
A future Jarvis Platform where a central Flask server acts as the brain for both the desktop app and the Android app
Every web browser and web server: the browser is the client, the web server serves HTML, CSS, and data
references: Roy Fielding — Architectural Styles and the Design of Network-based Software Architectures (Chapter 3)
Martin Fowler — Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture (Distribution Patterns)

architecture_patterns.name: MVC (Model-View-Controller)
architecture_patterns.description: Separates a user interface application into three interconnected components: the Model (data and business logic), the View (presentation), and the Controller (input handling). Each component has a single responsibility and communicates with the others through defined interfaces.
architecture_patterns.intent: Separate data management, user interface presentation, and user input handling so each can evolve independently and be tested in isolation.
architecture_patterns.structure: The Model holds application data and business logic. The View renders the Model's state to the user. The Controller receives user input, updates the Model, and selects the appropriate View. In many implementations the Controller is the entry point; it orchestrates interaction between Model and View.
components: Model — owns business data and rules; notifies observers when its state changes; has no knowledge of the View
View — renders Model state to the user; has no business logic; observes the Model for changes
Controller — receives user input; updates the Model; selects the View to render; thin orchestration only
advantages: Presentation and business logic are decoupled — the same Model can be rendered by multiple Views
Models can be tested without a UI
Views can be redesigned without touching business logic
Well understood pattern with strong framework support (Django, Rails, Spring MVC)
disadvantages: Controllers can become bloated with orchestration logic (Massive View Controller in iOS development)
The Model-View coupling through notifications can be difficult to trace in large applications
Not all UI paradigms map cleanly to MVC — it was designed for desktop GUIs and fits web applications imperfectly
State management complexity grows with application size
when_to_use: Web applications with clear separation between data and presentation
When using a framework that enforces MVC (Django, Rails, Spring MVC, ASP.NET MVC)
Applications where the same data must be rendered in multiple presentation formats
Desktop GUI applications with complex user interaction
when_not_to_use: Simple scripts or tools with no meaningful user interface
Highly reactive UIs where the Observer coupling between Model and View creates performance issues
When MVVM or MVP would provide a cleaner separation for the specific UI framework in use
examples: The Jarvis Desktop (apps/desktop/): window.py is the View, coordinator.py is the Controller, and JarvisCore with its subsystems is the Model
Django: models.py defines the Model, templates are Views, views.py functions are Controllers
Ruby on Rails: ActiveRecord is the Model, ERB templates are Views, ApplicationController subclasses are Controllers
references: Gang of Four — Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software (MVC discussion)
Martin Fowler — GUI Architectures (martinfowler.com, 2006)

architecture_patterns.name: MVVM (Model-View-ViewModel)
architecture_patterns.description: Separates a user interface into the Model (data and business logic), the View (UI rendering), and the ViewModel (a presentation-layer abstraction that exposes the Model's data in a form the View can bind to directly). The View never references the Model directly.
architecture_patterns.intent: Remove all UI logic from the View by introducing a ViewModel that the View binds to declaratively, enabling Views to be purely declarative markup with no code and ViewModels to be tested without a UI.
architecture_patterns.structure: The View binds to properties on the ViewModel using data binding. The ViewModel exposes observable properties and commands. When the Model changes, the ViewModel updates its properties and the View updates automatically through the binding. The ViewModel has no reference to the View.
components: Model — business data and rules; completely unaware of the ViewModel or View
ViewModel — presents Model data in View-friendly form; exposes observable properties and commands; contains all UI logic; no reference to the View
View — declarative UI markup bound to ViewModel properties; ideally contains no code-behind
Data Binding Framework — the mechanism that synchronises View and ViewModel properties automatically
advantages: ViewModels can be unit tested without instantiating any UI component
Views become pure markup — designers can work on the View while developers work on the ViewModel
The ViewModel's observable properties make UI state explicit and traceable
Multiple Views can bind to the same ViewModel
disadvantages: Data binding frameworks add complexity and can be difficult to debug
ViewModels can become large if not carefully bounded
The pattern requires a data binding mechanism — it does not work well without framework support
Overkill for simple forms or static UIs
when_to_use: Rich client applications with complex, reactive UI state (WPF, Xamarin, SwiftUI, Jetpack Compose)
When thorough ViewModel unit testing is a priority
Applications where the UI is driven by complex state machines rather than simple data display
Teams where UI designers and developers work in parallel on the same screens
when_not_to_use: Simple web applications where Django or Rails MVC is sufficient
When no data binding framework is available or the framework cost is not justified
Static or near-static UIs where the binding overhead is unnecessary
examples: The Jarvis Android app (JarvisApp2): each screen's state could be managed by a ViewModel that the Jetpack Compose UI binds to, keeping all UI logic testable
A WPF desktop application where MainWindowViewModel exposes Commands and ObservableProperties that the MainWindow.xaml binds to declaratively
SwiftUI applications where @ObservableObject ViewModels drive declarative View updates
references: John Gossman — Introduction to Model-View-ViewModel (Microsoft blog, 2005)
Martin Fowler — Presentation Model (martinfowler.com, 2004)

architecture_patterns.name: Repository-Centric Architecture
architecture_patterns.description: Centralises all shared state in a single authoritative repository that all components read from and write to. Components do not communicate directly — they express intent by updating the repository and react to changes in it.
architecture_patterns.intent: Eliminate direct component-to-component communication by making the repository the single source of truth, so every component always has a consistent view of the current state.
architecture_patterns.structure: A central repository holds all shared state. Components subscribe to changes in the repository or query it on demand. When a component needs to affect another, it writes to the repository rather than calling the other component directly. The repository raises events or notifications when its state changes.
components: Central Repository — the single authoritative store for all shared state
Readers — components that query the repository to make decisions
Writers — components that update the repository in response to events or user actions
Change Notifications — the mechanism by which the repository informs subscribers of state changes
advantages: A single source of truth eliminates inconsistent views of state across components
Components are decoupled from each other — they only depend on the repository interface
State changes are centralised and auditable
Testing is straightforward — put the system in a known state, trigger an action, assert the repository's new state
disadvantages: The repository can become a bottleneck if all components contend for it
The repository can become a God Object if too many unrelated concerns are stored together
Eventual consistency is difficult to reason about when multiple writers update the repository concurrently
Poor fit for distributed systems where state cannot be centralised
when_to_use: Applications with complex shared state that multiple components must observe consistently
IDEs, design tools, game engines, and other stateful desktop applications
When an audit trail of state changes is required
Single-process applications where a centralised store is practical
when_not_to_use: Distributed systems where the central repository becomes a network bottleneck or single point of failure
Simple applications where direct component communication is clearer than repository indirection
When different components have private state that should not be centralised
examples: Jarvis Knowledge Engine: all facts about the user are stored in a central KnowledgeRepository; the Agent, Skills, and Reasoning Engine all read from and write to it rather than passing state between themselves
Redux in React applications: all UI state lives in a central store; components dispatch actions and subscribe to store changes
Git itself: the .git directory is the central repository of all project history; all git commands read from and write to it
references: Martin Fowler — Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture (Repository pattern)
Eric Evans — Domain-Driven Design (Repositories chapter)

architecture_patterns.name: Plugin Architecture
architecture_patterns.description: Defines a stable core application and an extension mechanism that allows third-party or community-developed plugins to add capability without modifying the core. The core defines extension points; plugins implement them.
architecture_patterns.intent: Allow the application's capabilities to be extended indefinitely by independent developers without modifying or redeploying the core, while keeping the core stable and the extension points well-defined.
architecture_patterns.structure: The core defines Plugin Interfaces (extension points) and a Plugin Registry that discovers and loads plugins at startup or runtime. Each plugin implements the Plugin Interface and registers itself. The core calls plugins through the interface without knowing their concrete types.
components: Core Application — stable; defines extension points; never modified to add new capability
Plugin Interface — the contract that all plugins must implement
Plugin Registry — discovers, loads, and manages the lifecycle of plugins
Plugin — a self-contained capability module that implements the Plugin Interface
Plugin Loader — discovers plugins from a directory, configuration file, or package registry
advantages: The core remains stable and well-tested while capabilities grow indefinitely
Third-party developers can extend the application without access to core source code
Plugins can be developed, deployed, and updated independently of the core
The community marketplace model — users can share and rate plugins
Unused capabilities carry no runtime cost if plugins are loaded on demand
disadvantages: Plugin interfaces must be designed carefully — breaking changes affect all plugins
Plugin quality control is difficult — a poorly written plugin can destabilise the core
Security boundaries between the core and plugins require explicit design
Plugin discovery and loading adds startup complexity
when_to_use: Applications where the exact set of required capabilities cannot be known in advance
Platforms intended to support a developer ecosystem or community marketplace
Applications where different deployments need different capability sets
IDEs, editors, build tools, CMS platforms, and any extensible application
when_not_to_use: Applications with a fixed, well-known capability set where extensibility adds no value
When plugin quality control and security are too difficult to manage for the team's maturity
Simple applications where the plugin loading infrastructure exceeds the application's own complexity
examples: The Jarvis Community Marketplace vision: users build tools for Jarvis by implementing a JarvisToolPlugin interface; the core loads and executes them without modification
The Jarvis Engineering Academy itself: new knowledge bases (patterns, anti-patterns, architecture patterns) are plugins that extend the Academy without modifying the loader or service
VS Code: the editor is a stable core; thousands of extensions implement the Extension API to add language support, themes, and tools
references: Robert C. Martin — Clean Architecture (Chapter 15: What is Architecture? — Plugin Architecture)
Martin Fowler — Plugin (martinfowler.com, P of EAA)

architecture_patterns.name: Pipeline Architecture
architecture_patterns.description: Structures processing as a sequence of independent stages where the output of each stage is the input of the next. Each stage performs a single transformation. Stages can be composed, reordered, and replaced without affecting other stages.
architecture_patterns.intent: Make complex data processing clear, composable, and independently testable by decomposing it into a linear sequence of single-responsibility transformation stages.
architecture_patterns.structure: A Source produces data. A series of Filters (stages) transform the data in sequence. A Sink consumes the final output. Each stage reads from its input, transforms, and writes to its output. Stages are connected by Pipes (queues, streams, or function calls). Stages share no state with each other.
components: Source — produces the initial data entering the pipeline
Filters (Stages) — independent transformation steps; each has one responsibility
Pipes — the connections between stages that carry data (function return values, queues, streams)
Sink — consumes the pipeline's final output
Pipeline Coordinator — assembles stages into the correct sequence and manages execution
advantages: Each stage is independently testable with its own unit tests
Stages can be reordered, added, or removed without affecting others
New processing requirements are met by adding a stage, not modifying existing ones
Pipeline execution is easy to trace and instrument — each stage produces observable output
Stages can be parallelised if they are independent
disadvantages: All data must pass through every stage even when only some stages are relevant — inefficient for sparse data
Complex branching logic does not map naturally to a linear pipeline
Error handling across multiple stages requires careful design
Pipelines with many stages can be difficult to understand at a glance
when_to_use: Data transformation, ETL, and batch processing systems
Compiler and language processing toolchains (lex, parse, analyse, optimise, emit)
Request processing middleware stacks (authentication, authorisation, logging, routing)
When a complex process can be cleanly decomposed into sequential single-responsibility stages
when_not_to_use: Interactive request-response applications where latency matters and pipeline overhead is significant
Processes with complex branching that cannot be expressed as a linear sequence of stages
When stages need to share state — stateful stages couple the pipeline and break composability
examples: The Jarvis Engineering Pipeline (Genesis-016): Repository Catalogue → Git Awareness → Planning Engine → EngineeringPlan → Guardrails → Chief Approval → Testing — each stage is independent and testable
The Jarvis Agent request pipeline: classification → behaviour detection → memory detection → intent routing → skill execution → response delivery — each is a discrete stage
Unix shell pipelines: cat file.txt | grep error | sort | uniq -c — each command is a stage
references: Martin Fowler — Pipes and Filters (martinfowler.com, Enterprise Integration Patterns)
Gregor Hohpe, Bobby Woolf — Enterprise Integration Patterns (Chapter 2: Messaging Channels)
